content.text: Now wire `reachable_functions` into the chooser:

input.old_string:
blocks = parse_hotblocks(args.plugin_log)
    if not blocks:
        print(f"ERROR: no hotblocks parsed from {args.plugin_log}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    # Find the top block whose containing function is reachable from --function.
    # Reachability is approximated by: same function, OR any function in the
    # objdump (libnn-only objdump means every fn there is link-reachable).
    # We pick the first hotblock whose containing fn is anywhere in the
    # functions list -- i.e. we trust the link edit, not the call graph.
    chosen = None
    for blk in blocks[: args.top_k * 4]:
        fn = fn_at(functions, blk["pc"])
        if fn is None:
            continue
        chosen = (blk, fn)
        break

    if chosen is None:
        print("ERROR: no hot block maps to any function in objdump", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
input.new_string:
blocks = parse_hotblocks(args.plugin_log)
    if not blocks:
        print(f"ERROR: no hotblocks parsed from {args.plugin_log}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    # Restrict candidate hot blocks to functions reachable from --function.
    # Without this filter, the test harness's fread loop dominates ecount.
    in_scope = reachable_functions(insns, functions, args.function)
    if args.function not in in_scope:
        print(f"ERROR: function <{args.function}> not found in objdump",
              file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    chosen = None
    for blk in blocks:
        fn = fn_at(functions, blk["pc"])
        if fn is None or fn[2] not in in_scope:
            continue
        chosen = (blk, fn)
        break

    if chosen is None:
        print(f"ERROR: no hot block lands inside <{args.function}> or its callees",
              file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. content.text: Bug: `nds.vd4dots.vv` isn't being classified as RMW because of the `nds.` prefix, so v0..v3 (accumulators) are missing from live-ins. Fix:

input.old_string:
def classify_rw(insn):
    """
    Return (writes, reads) sets of registers (kind, name) tuples.
    `writes` = registers definitely written by this instruction.
    `reads`  = registers read.
    """
    mnem = insn["mnemonic"]
    operands = insn["operands"]
    regs = extract_regs(operands)
input.new_string:
def classify_rw(insn):
    """
    Return (writes, reads) sets of registers (kind, name) tuples.
    `writes` = registers definitely written by this instruction.
    `reads`  = registers read.
    """
    mnem = insn["mnemonic"]
    # Andes vendor extensions appear as `nds.<base>` (e.g. nds.vd4dots.vv,
    # nds.lea.h, nds.bfos). For r/w classification we want to match against
    # the base mnemonic. Strip the vendor namespace -- semantics of all the
    # nds.* ops in libnn follow the standard "rd is first operand" rule with
    # one exception (nds.vd4dots), which we keep in RMW_VECTOR_PREFIXES below.
    if mnem.startswith("nds."):
        mnem = mnem[4:]
    operands = insn["operands"]
    regs = extract_regs(operands)

- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Emit a self-contained .S harness that runs the extracted hot inner loop body
N times under mcycle measurement.

Inputs:
  --analysis    : JSON from find_hot_loop.py
  --output      : path to write the .S file
  --warmup      : warm-up iterations (unmeasured, settle pipeline) [default 5]
  --iters       : measured iteration count [default 100]
  --buf-size    : input/weight scratch buffer size in bytes [default 65536]

Output:
  A complete RISC-V assembly source file with:
    .data    -- scratch buffers for vector loads
    .text    -- _start that:
                  1. allocates a small stack
                  2. initializes scalar live-ins to point at scratch buffers /
                     reasonable counts, vector live-ins via vle from scratch
                  3. runs <warmup> iterations of the loop body
                  4. wraps <iters> iterations in csrr mcycle / csrr mcycle
                  5. exits via a `_exit:` label users can hook in their TB

Design notes:
  - The captured loop body is inlined verbatim, EXCEPT:
      * the backward branch at loop_end_pc is stripped (we wrap with our own
        outer counter)
      * any other branches (mid-loop early-exit) are kept; the user is
        expected to set up live-in counts so those branches don't fire
  - Each measured iteration RESETS scalar pointer/count live-ins so the body
    walks the same buffer every time. Without reset, pointers would walk off
    the buffer and counts would go negative within a few iters.
  - Vector live-ins are initialized ONCE (in setup) and the loop body is
    expected to leave them in a consistent state. For software-pipelined
    bodies that consume v_lo and produce v_hi (or vice versa), this works
    because the body's writes restore the inputs the next iter expects.

The harness is written in standalone format -- no semihosting calls in the
critical region. Cycle deltas are stashed in known memory locations so a
Verilator testbench can scrape them by address, or a smoke-test on QEMU can
print them via post-region semihosting.
"""

import argparse
import json
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path


# Regex to peel "ADDR: HEX MNEMONIC OPERANDS" out of an objdump line.
INSN_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*([0-9a-f]+):\s+([0-9a-f]+)\s+(\S+)(?:\s+(.*))?$")


def parse_insn_line(line):
    """Return dict {addr, mnemonic, operands, raw} or None."""
    m = INSN_RE.match(line)
    if not m:
        return None
    return {
        "addr":     int(m.group(1), 16),
        "mnemonic": m.group(3),
        "operands": (m.group(4) or "").strip(),
        "raw":      line,
    }


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Live-in initialization
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Default policy: scalar pointer-like names (a0..a7, t0..t6, s0..s11) get
# pointed at scratch_in / scratch_wt / scratch_out depending on a heuristic
# based on the order they appear in the loop body's first vle8.v ops.

# We don't try to reason about WHICH scalar is which buffer -- that's an
# overconstrained problem and the user knows their kernel best. Instead we
# emit ALL live-in scalars pointing at scratch_in by default, and add a
# comment block recommending the user override per their buffer layout.

# Vector live-ins: load from scratch_in for v8..v23 (consumer half), zero v0
# (accumulator pattern). Adjust per kernel.

SCALAR_PTR_HINT = {
    # If you see these names pointing at output, edit the harness:
    "a1": "out",  # AAPCS: first arg is dst-ish; for libnn unroll4 a1 is dst
    "a2": "in",
    "a0": "in",
}


def emit_live_in_init(live_ins, label_in="scratch_in", label_wt="scratch_wt",
                      label_out="scratch_out"):
    """
    Emit asm lines that establish each live-in scalar/vector to a known value.
    Buffer assignment is HEURISTIC -- the user is expected to read the
    generated comments and adjust.
    """
    out = []
    out.append("    # ---- live-in initialization ----")
    out.append("    # NOTE: scalar pointer assignments are heuristic. Read")
    out.append("    # the loop body and adjust labels (scratch_in/_wt/_out)")
    out.append("    # if a register actually points at a different buffer.")

    # Scalar live-ins.
    for name in live_ins["scalar"]:
        if name in ("zero", "x0"):
            continue
        if name == "sp":
            # We allocate sp ourselves below.
            continue
        if name in ("ra", "tp", "gp"):
            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0   # neutral init")
            continue
        # Loop-bound-like names get a small positive constant.
        # Stride-like names get the reset constant on each measured iter.
        # Default: point at scratch_in. User edits as needed.
        if name in ("s1", "a3", "a4", "a5", "t6"):
            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0x100   # heuristic count; edit if loop bound")
        else:
            buf = "scratch_out" if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "out" else \
                  "scratch_wt"  if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "wt"  else \
                  "scratch_in"
            out.append(f"    la {name}, {buf}   # heuristic pointer init")

    # Vector live-ins.
    if live_ins["vector"]:
        out.append("    # vector live-ins: zero accumulators (v0..v15 if RMW), load v16+ from scratch_in")
        out.append("    vsetvli t0, x0, e8, m8, tu, mu   # max VL for fill")
        # Group v0..v7 and v8..v15 are typically accumulators -- zero them.
        # v16..v23, v24..v31 are typically activations/weights -- load.
        for vname in live_ins["vector"]:
            n = int(vname[1:])
            if n < 16:
                out.append(f"    vand.vi {vname}, {vname}, 0   # zero accumulator")
            else:
                out.append(f"    vle8.v {vname}, (a0)   # load from scratch_in (heuristic)")
        # Re-set vtype to whatever the loop body expects on entry.
        out.append("    vsetvli x0, x0, e8, m1, tu, mu   # match loop entry vtype (override if different)")

    return out


def emit_live_in_reset(live_ins):
    """
    Emit asm to reset SCALAR live-ins each measured iteration. We don't reset
    vector regs each iter -- they're either accumulators (we want the running
    sum) or pipeline-carried (the body's writes restore them). Resetting vector
    regs would defeat the SW-pipeline behavior we're trying to measure.
    """
    out = []
    out.append("    # reset scalar live-ins each iter (so pointers don't walk off buffer)")
    for name in live_ins["scalar"]:
        if name in ("zero", "x0", "sp", "ra", "tp", "gp"):
            continue
        if name in ("s1", "a3", "a4", "a5", "t6"):
            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0x100")
        else:
            buf = "scratch_out" if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "out" else \
                  "scratch_wt"  if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "wt"  else \
                  "scratch_in"
            out.append(f"    la {name}, {buf}")
    return out


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Loop body emission
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def emit_loop_body(loop_lines, loop_end_pc):
    """
    Convert objdump lines back to assembleable instructions. We KEEP the raw
    text after the hex column (`mnemonic operands`) and DROP the backward
    branch at loop_end_pc.

    objdump emits `nds.vd4dots.vv v0, v24, v28` -- the assembler accepts the
    same syntax for the toolchain's matching `-march`, so we just pass it
    through.
    """
    out = []
    for raw in loop_lines:
        ins = parse_insn_line(raw)
        if ins is None:
            continue
        # Strip the closing backward branch -- our outer counter takes its place.
        if ins["addr"] == loop_end_pc:
            out.append(f"    # (stripped backward branch: {ins['mnemonic']} {ins['operands']})")
            continue
        # Comment lines emitted by objdump like "# 0x..." should be kept; they're harmless.
        # Some operands have a trailing comment "<sym>" -- the assembler accepts these.
        out.append(f"    {ins['mnemonic']} {ins['operands']}".rstrip())
    return out


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Whole-file emission
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

HEADER = """\
###############################################################################
# Hot-inner-kernel harness
#
# Auto-generated by hot-inner-kernel-extractor. Do NOT edit the generated body
# directly; re-run the extractor with adjusted args instead.
#
# Function : {fn}
# Hot block: {hot_pc}  (ecount={ecount})
# Loop     : {loop_start} .. {loop_end}  ({body_count} insns)
# Live-ins : {n_scalar} scalar, {n_vector} vector
###############################################################################

    .section .data
    .align 6
scratch_in:
    .skip {bufsize}
    .align 6
scratch_wt:
    .skip {bufsize}
    .align 6
scratch_out:
    .skip {bufsize}

    .align 3
cycle_total:    .dword 0   # measured cycles for {iters} iterations
cycle_warmup:   .dword 0   # cycles for warmup (informational)

    .section .text
    .globl _start
_start:
    # Use a fresh small stack -- the harness has no caller.
    la sp, _stack_top
"""

WARMUP = """\

    # ---- warmup: settle pipeline state, do NOT measure ----
    li t0, {warmup}
warmup_loop:
{reset}
{body}
    addi t0, t0, -1
    bnez t0, warmup_loop
"""

MEASURE = """\

    # ---- measurement window ----
    csrr s10, mcycle
    li t0, {iters}
measure_loop:
{reset}
{body}
    addi t0, t0, -1
    bnez t0, measure_loop
    csrr s11, mcycle
    sub  s11, s11, s10
    la   t1, cycle_total
    sd   s11, 0(t1)
"""

EXIT = """\

    # ---- exit ----
    # Verilator testbenches: scrape `cycle_total` from memory.
    # QEMU smoke-test: emit semihosting EXIT_EXTENDED with code 0.
_exit:
1:  j 1b   # spin; testbench observes cycle_total at this PC

    .section .bss
    .align 4
    .skip 4096
_stack_top:
"""


def emit_harness(analysis, warmup, iters, bufsize):
    live_ins = analysis["live_in_regs"]
    loop_end_pc = int(analysis["loop_end_pc"], 16)

    init_lines = emit_live_in_init(live_ins)
    reset_lines = emit_live_in_reset(live_ins)
    body_lines = emit_loop_body(analysis["loop_body_lines"], loop_end_pc)

    indent = lambda lines: "\n".join(lines)
    header = HEADER.format(
        fn=analysis["containing_fn"],
        hot_pc=analysis["hot_block_pc"],
        ecount=analysis["hot_block_ecount"],
        loop_start=analysis["loop_start_pc"],
        loop_end=analysis["loop_end_pc"],
        body_count=analysis["loop_body_count"],
        n_scalar=len(live_ins["scalar"]),
        n_vector=len(live_ins["vector"]),
        bufsize=bufsize,
        iters=iters,
    )
    body_text   = indent(body_lines)
    reset_text  = indent(reset_lines)

    parts = [
        header,
        indent(init_lines),
        WARMUP.format(warmup=warmup, reset=reset_text, body=body_text),
        MEASURE.format(iters=iters, reset=reset_text, body=body_text),
        EXIT,
    ]
    return "\n".join(parts)


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--analysis", required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--output",   required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--warmup",   type=int, default=5)
    ap.add_argument("--iters",    type=int, default=100)
    ap.add_argument("--buf-size", type=int, default=65536)
    args = ap.parse_args()

    analysis = json.loads(args.analysis.read_text())
    text = emit_harness(analysis, args.warmup, args.iters, args.buf_size)
    args.output.write_text(text)
    print(f"[ok] harness written to {args.output} "
          f"({args.iters} measured iters, {args.warmup} warmup, "
          f"buf={args.buf_size}B)")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Hot block: {hot_pc}  (ecount={ecount})
# Loop     : {loop_start} .. {loop_end}  ({body_count} insns)
# Live-ins : {n_scalar} scalar, {n_vector} vector
###############################################################################

    .section .data
    .align 6
scratch_in:
    .skip {bufsize}
    .align 6
scratch_wt:
    .skip {bufsize}
    .align 6
scratch_out:
    .skip {bufsize}

    .align 3
cycle_total:    .dword 0   # measured cycles for {iters} iterations
cycle_warmup:   .dword 0   # cycles for warmup (informational)

    .section .text
    .globl _start
_start:
    # Use a fresh small stack -- the harness has no caller.
    la sp, _stack_top
"""

WARMUP = """\

    # ---- warmup: settle pipeline state, do NOT measure ----
    li t0, {warmup}
warmup_loop:
{reset}
{body}
    addi t0, t0, -1
    bnez t0, warmup_loop
"""

MEASURE = """\

    # ---- measurement window ----
    csrr s10, mcycle
    li t0, {iters}
measure_loop:
{reset}
{body}
    addi t0, t0, -1
    bnez t0, measure_loop
    csrr s11, mcycle
    sub  s11, s11, s10
    la   t1, cycle_total
    sd   s11, 0(t1)
"""

EXIT = """\

    # ---- exit ----
    # Verilator testbenches: scrape `cycle_total` from memory.
    # QEMU smoke-test: emit semihosting EXIT_EXTENDED with code 0.
_exit:
1:  j 1b   # spin; testbench observes cycle_total at this PC

    .section .bss
    .align 4
    .skip 4096
_stack_top:
"""


def emit_harness(analysis, warmup, iters, bufsize):
    live_ins = analysis["live_in_regs"]
    loop_end_pc = int(analysis["loop_end_pc"], 16)

    init_lines = emit_live_in_init(live_ins)
    reset_lines = emit_live_in_reset(live_ins)
    body_lines = emit_loop_body(analysis["loop_body_lines"], loop_end_pc)

    indent = lambda lines: "\n".join(lines)
    header = HEADER.format(
        fn=analysis["containing_fn"],
        hot_pc=analysis["hot_block_pc"],
        ecount=analysis["hot_block_ecount"],
        loop_start=analysis["loop_start_pc"],
        loop_end=analysis["loop_end_pc"],
        body_count=analysis["loop_body_count"],
        n_scalar=len(live_ins["scalar"]),
        n_vector=len(live_ins["vector"]),
        bufsize=bufsize,
        iters=iters,
    )
    body_text   = indent(body_lines)
    reset_text  = indent(reset_lines)

    parts = [
        header,
        indent(init_lines),
        WARMUP.format(warmup=warmup, reset=reset_text, body=body_text),
        MEASURE.format(iters=iters, reset=reset_text, body=body_text),
        EXIT,
    ]
    return "\n".join(parts)


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--analysis", required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--output",   required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--warmup",   type=int, default=5)
    ap.add_argument("--iters",    type=int, default=100)
    ap.add_argument("--buf-size", type=int, default=65536)
    args = ap.parse_args()

    analysis = json.loads(args.analysis.read_text())
    text = emit_harness(analysis, args.warmup, args.iters, args.buf_size)
    args.output.write_text(text)
    print(f"[ok] harness written to {args.output} "
          f"({args.iters} measured iters, {args.warmup} warmup, "
          f"buf={args.buf_size}B)")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# End-to-end orchestrator: given a libnn function, produce
#   1. work/<fn>/analysis.json     -- find_hot_loop.py output
#   2. work/<fn>/analysis.md       -- human-readable report
#   3. work/<fn>/harness.S         -- compilable harness
#
# Steps:
#   1. resolve <fn> to its source file and unit test name
#   2. (re)build libnn.a + perf objdump if needed
#   3. run QEMU + hotblocks plugin against the unit test
#   4. analyze hot block + loop boundaries + live-ins
#   5. emit harness .S
#   6. write a markdown report
#
# Usage:
#   extract_kernel.sh <function_name> [--iters N] [--warmup N] [--buf-size N]
################################################################################
set -uo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
OPT_SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "${SCRIPT_DIR}/../../optimize/script" && pwd)"

FN=""
ITERS=100
WARMUP=5
BUFSIZE=65536

while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --iters)    ITERS="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --warmup)   WARMUP="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        --buf-size) BUFSIZE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        -*)         echo "Unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 1 ;;
        *)          FN="$1"; shift ;;
    esac
done

if [ -z "${FN}" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <function_name> [--iters N] [--warmup N] [--buf-size N]" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# shellcheck disable=SC1091
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"

OUT_DIR="${CFG_HIK_WORK_DIR}/${FN}"
mkdir -p "${OUT_DIR}"

echo "============================================================"
echo "  hot-inner-kernel-extractor  --  ${FN}"
echo "============================================================"

# ---- Step 1: resolve test name ---------------------------------------------
# Convention: t_<short>.c where <short> is the function with riscv_nn_ stripped.
SHORT="${FN#riscv_nn_}"
TEST_SRC="${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func/t_${SHORT}.c"
if [ ! -f "${TEST_SRC}" ]; then
    # Some tests use the bare function name without the prefix strip.
    TEST_SRC_ALT="${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func/t_${FN}.c"
    if [ -f "${TEST_SRC_ALT}" ]; then
        SHORT="${FN}"
        TEST_SRC="${TEST_SRC_ALT}"
    else
        echo "ERROR: no unit test found for ${FN} -- looked at:" >&2
        echo "  ${TEST_SRC}" >&2
        echo "  ${TEST_SRC_ALT}" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi
echo "[step 1] test name: ${SHORT}  src: ${TEST_SRC}"

# ---- Step 2: ensure libnn.a + perf objdump are fresh -----------------------
LIBNN="${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.a"
PERF_OBJDUMP="${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_${SHORT}_perf.objdump"

if [ ! -f "${LIBNN}" ]; then
    echo "[step 2] libnn.a missing -- running optimize/build_libnn.sh"
    /usr/bin/bash "${OPT_SCRIPT_DIR}/build_libnn.sh"
fi

if [ ! -f "${PERF_OBJDUMP}" ]; then
    echo "[step 2] perf objdump missing -- running run_fpga_test.sh to generate it"
    echo "         (this also produces .adx for QEMU; if the FPGA is unreachable"
    echo "          you can stub this by manually running the perf compile step)"
    /usr/bin/bash "${OPT_SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh" "${SHORT}" || {
        echo "WARN: run_fpga_test.sh failed; falling back to perf-objdump-only build" >&2
        # Try a minimal compile that produces the perf objdump without uploading.
        # This path is best-effort -- the user should have run the optimize loop
        # at least once before using this skill.
        if [ ! -f "${PERF_OBJDUMP}" ]; then
            echo "ERROR: cannot produce ${PERF_OBJDUMP} without FPGA access." >&2
            echo "       Run the /optimize skill on this function once, then retry." >&2
            exit 1
        fi
    }
fi
echo "[step 2] objdump: ${PERF_OBJDUMP}"

# ---- Step 3: run QEMU + hotblocks ------------------------------------------
echo "[step 3] running hotblocks profile"
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_hotblocks.sh" "${SHORT}"
PLUGIN_LOG="${CFG_HIK_WORK_DIR}/${SHORT}.plugin.log"

# ---- Step 4: analyze --------------------------------------------------------
echo "[step 4] analyzing hot loop + live-ins"
ANALYSIS_JSON="${OUT_DIR}/analysis.json"
python3 "${SCRIPT_DIR}/find_hot_loop.py" \
    --plugin-log "${PLUGIN_LOG}" \
    --objdump    "${PERF_OBJDUMP}" \
    --function   "${FN}" \
    > "${ANALYSIS_JSON}"

# ---- Step 5: emit harness ---------------------------------------------------
echo "[step 5] emitting harness"
HARNESS_S="${OUT_DIR}/harness.S"
python3 "${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_harness.py" \
    --analysis "${ANALYSIS_JSON}" \
    --output   "${HARNESS_S}" \
    --iters    "${ITERS}" \
    --warmup   "${WARMUP}" \
    --buf-size "${BUFSIZE}"

# ---- Step 6: write markdown report -----------------------------------------
ANALYSIS_MD="${OUT_DIR}/analysis.md"
python3 "${SCRIPT_DIR}/write_report.py" \
    --analysis "${ANALYSIS_JSON}" \
    --harness  "${HARNESS_S}" \
    --output   "${ANALYSIS_MD}"

echo
echo "============================================================"
echo "  DONE"
echo "============================================================"
echo "  analysis.json : ${ANALYSIS_JSON}"
echo "  analysis.md   : ${ANALYSIS_MD}"
echo "  harness.S     : ${HARNESS_S}"
echo
echo "  Next: assemble harness.S with"
echo "    \${CFG_CC} \${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE} -nostdlib -static -T<linker.ld> \\"
echo "        ${HARNESS_S} -o harness.elf"
echo "  then run on Verilator / QEMU and read \`cycle_total\` from memory."
echo "============================================================"

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Now the report writer:

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Render a human-readable Markdown report from analysis.json + harness.S.

The report is the primary user-facing artifact. It explains WHAT was found
(hot block, loop boundary, live-ins) and WHY each piece was selected, so the
user can spot-check the extraction before trusting the harness.
"""

import argparse
import json
from pathlib import Path


REPORT_TEMPLATE = """\
# Hot inner kernel: `{fn}`

## Summary
- Hot block PC: `{hot_pc}` (executed **{ecount}** times)
- Containing function: `{fn}` (starts at `{fn_start}`)
- Inner loop: `{loop_start}` .. `{loop_end}` ({body_count} instructions)
- Prologue captured: {prologue_count} instructions before loop entry
- Live-in regs: {n_scalar} scalar, {n_vector} vector
- Producer subset: {producer_count} instructions (the prologue insns that
  transitively produce live-in values)

## Why this block

The `hotblocks` TCG plugin recorded translation-block exec counts during a
QEMU run of the unit test. After filtering to the call graph reachable from
`{fn}` (i.e. excluding the test harness's I/O loops), the hottest block by
exec count is `{hot_pc}`. The smallest backward branch enclosing this block
defines the inner loop boundary.

### Top hot blocks (after call-graph filter)
| PC | exec_count | inst_count | function |
|---|---:|---:|---|
{top_table}

## Live-in registers

These are the registers the loop body **reads before writing**. They MUST be
established by the harness prologue before the measurement window starts.

- **Scalar:** {scalar_list}
- **Vector:** {vector_list}

The `vd4dots`-style accumulator pattern means vector accumulator regs (e.g.
v0..v15) are read-modify-written each iteration -- they're live-ins because
the loop expects an initial accumulator value (typically zero).

For SW-pipelined kernels, two distinct vector register groups appear as
live-ins (e.g. v16..v23 AND v24..v31): one group is consumed in the first
half of the body and produced in the second, the other group vice versa. The
running iteration depends on values produced by the previous iteration -- so
warm-up iterations are essential.

## Loop body

```asm
{loop_body}
```

## Prologue (verbatim, from function entry)

If you prefer a conservative-correct harness that replays the actual function
prologue, copy this verbatim. The auto-emitted harness uses a leaner
hand-built init instead. You may want to splice values from this prologue
into the harness when the heuristic init is wrong.

<details><summary>Show {prologue_count} prologue instructions</summary>

```asm
{prologue_lines}
```
</details>

## Live-in producers (pruned prologue)

These are the prologue instructions that transitively write at least one
live-in register. This is a sound subset of the verbatim prologue -- enough
to establish all loop entry state, with dead code dropped.

<details><summary>Show {producer_count} producer instructions</summary>

```asm
{producer_lines}
```
</details>

## Generated harness

Path: `{harness_path}`

The harness:
1. Allocates scratch buffers in `.data`
2. Initializes all live-in registers (heuristic -- see warning below)
3. Runs **warmup** iterations (unmeasured) to settle the pipeline
4. Wraps **N_ITERS** iterations in `csrr mcycle` reads
5. Stores the cycle delta to `cycle_total` in memory and spins at `_exit:`

### Warning: heuristic init

The harness defaults all live-in scalar pointers to `scratch_in`, all
"counter-like" scalars to `0x100`, all low-numbered vector regs (v0..v15) to
zero, and all high-numbered vector regs (v16..v31) loaded from `scratch_in`.

This works for many GEMM-shaped inner loops, but you should READ the loop
body and adjust if:
- A scalar reg actually points at the *output* buffer (would corrupt its
  iteration if pointed at `scratch_in`).
- A scalar reg is a *count* whose initial value drives a forward early-exit
  branch in the loop body.
- A vector reg is read with non-zero semantics (e.g. a broadcast constant).

Look for the comment `# heuristic` lines in the harness and edit them.

### Building & running

```bash
# Assemble (use the same toolchain + base CFLAGS as libnn)
${{CFG_CC}} ${{CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}} -nostdlib -static \\
    -T <your_linker_script.ld> \\
    {harness_path} -o harness.elf

# Verilator: run elf, scrape `cycle_total` symbol from elf, read its
# value from simulated memory at sim end.
# QEMU sanity check (no cycle accuracy):
${{CFG_QEMU_BIN}} -M ${{CFG_QEMU_MACHINE}} -cpu ${{CFG_QEMU_CPU}} \\
    -nographic -semihosting -bios harness.elf
```

### Tuning the iter count

Pipeline steady state is usually reached in 3-5 iters on a 5-stage in-order
machine like AX45MPV. Compare cycles/iter at N=10 vs N=100; if they match
within 1-2% you've confirmed steady state and N=100 is plenty.
"""


def fmt_top_table(top_blocks):
    rows = []
    for b in top_blocks:
        rows.append(f"| `{b['pc']}` | {b['ecount']} | {b['icount']} | `{b['fn']}` |")
    return "\n".join(rows)


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--analysis", required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--harness",  required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--output",   required=True, type=Path)
    args = ap.parse_args()

    a = json.loads(args.analysis.read_text())
    li = a["live_in_regs"]

    text = REPORT_TEMPLATE.format(
        fn=a["containing_fn"],
        hot_pc=a["hot_block_pc"],
        ecount=a["hot_block_ecount"],
        fn_start=a["containing_fn_start"],
        loop_start=a["loop_start_pc"],
        loop_end=a["loop_end_pc"],
        body_count=a["loop_body_count"],
        prologue_count=a["prologue_count"],
        producer_count=a["live_in_producers_count"],
        n_scalar=len(li["scalar"]),
        n_vector=len(li["vector"]),
        top_table=fmt_top_table(a["top_hotblocks"]),
        scalar_list=", ".join(f"`{r}`" for r in li["scalar"]) or "(none)",
        vector_list=", ".join(f"`{r}`" for r in li["vector"]) or "(none)",
        loop_body="\n".join(a["loop_body_lines"]),
        prologue_lines="\n".join(a["prologue_lines"]),
        producer_lines="\n".join(a["live_in_producers"]),
        harness_path=str(args.harness),
    )
    args.output.write_text(text)
    print(f"[ok] report written to {args.output}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Render a human-readable Markdown report from analysis.json + harness.S.

The report is the primary user-facing artifact. It explains WHAT was found
(hot block, loop boundary, live-ins) and WHY each piece was selected, so the
user can spot-check the extraction before trusting the harness.
"""

import argparse
import json
from pathlib import Path


REPORT_TEMPLATE = """\
# Hot inner kernel: `{fn}`

## Summary
- Hot block PC: `{hot_pc}` (executed **{ecount}** times)
- Containing function: `{fn}` (starts at `{fn_start}`)
- Inner loop: `{loop_start}` .. `{loop_end}` ({body_count} instructions)
- Prologue captured: {prologue_count} instructions before loop entry
- Live-in regs: {n_scalar} scalar, {n_vector} vector
- Producer subset: {producer_count} instructions (the prologue insns that
  transitively produce live-in values)

## Why this block

The `hotblocks` TCG plugin recorded translation-block exec counts during a
QEMU run of the unit test. After filtering to the call graph reachable from
`{fn}` (i.e. excluding the test harness's I/O loops), the hottest block by
exec count is `{hot_pc}`. The smallest backward branch enclosing this block
defines the inner loop boundary.

### Top hot blocks (after call-graph filter)
| PC | exec_count | inst_count | function |
|---|---:|---:|---|
{top_table}

## Live-in registers

These are the registers the loop body **reads before writing**. They MUST be
established by the harness prologue before the measurement window starts.

- **Scalar:** {scalar_list}
- **Vector:** {vector_list}

The `vd4dots`-style accumulator pattern means vector accumulator regs (e.g.
v0..v15) are read-modify-written each iteration -- they're live-ins because
the loop expects an initial accumulator value (typically zero).

For SW-pipelined kernels, two distinct vector register groups appear as
live-ins (e.g. v16..v23 AND v24..v31): one group is consumed in the first
half of the body and produced in the second, the other group vice versa. The
running iteration depends on values produced by the previous iteration -- so
warm-up iterations are essential.

## Loop body

```asm
{loop_body}
```

## Prologue (verbatim, from function entry)

If you prefer a conservative-correct harness that replays the actual function
prologue, copy this verbatim. The auto-emitted harness uses a leaner
hand-built init instead. You may want to splice values from this prologue
into the harness when the heuristic init is wrong.

<details><summary>Show {prologue_count} prologue instructions</summary>

```asm
{prologue_lines}
```
</details>

## Live-in producers (pruned prologue)

These are the prologue instructions that transitively write at least one
live-in register. This is a sound subset of the verbatim prologue -- enough
to establish all loop entry state, with dead code dropped.

<details><summary>Show {producer_count} producer instructions</summary>

```asm
{producer_lines}
```
</details>

## Generated harness

Path: `{harness_path}`

1. Allocates scratch buffers in `.data`

5. Stores the cycle delta to `cycle_total` in memory and spins at `_exit:`

"counter-like" scalars to `0x100`, all low-numbered vector regs (v0..v15) to
zero, and all high-numbered vector regs (v16..v31) loaded from `scratch_in`.

body and adjust if:
- A scalar reg actually points at the *output* buffer (would corrupt its

  branch in the loop body.
- A vector reg is read with non-zero semantics (e.g. a broadcast constant).

Look for the comment `# heuristic` lines in the harness and edit them.

```bash
    {harness_path} -o harness.elf

# Verilator: run elf, scrape `cycle_total` symbol from elf, read its

${{CFG_QEMU_BIN}} -M ${{CFG_QEMU_MACHINE}} -cpu ${{CFG_QEMU_CPU}} \\

```

### Tuning the iter count

Pipeline steady state is usually reached in 3-5 iters on a 5-stage in-order
machine like AX45MPV. Compare cycles/iter at N=10 vs N=100; if they match
within 1-2% you've confirmed steady state and N=100 is plenty.
"""


def fmt_top_table(top_blocks):

    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()

        hot_pc=a["hot_block_pc"],

        fn_start=a["containing_fn_start"],
        loop_start=a["loop_start_pc"],
        loop_end=a["loop_end_pc"],

        n_scalar=len(li["scalar"]),
        n_vector=len(li["vector"]),

        vector_list=", ".join(f"`{r}`" for r in li["vector"]) or "(none)",
        loop_body="\n".join(a["loop_body_lines"]),

input.content:
---
name: hot-inner-kernel-extractor
description: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
---

# hot-inner-kernel-extractor

Produces a self-contained, cycle-measurable assembly harness from a libnn
function's hot inner loop. Designed for cases where running the full function
on a cycle-accurate simulator (Verilator at ~9 kHz, RTL emulators, etc.) is
too slow to reach steady state -- by extracting just the inner kernel and
wrapping it in a counted measurement loop, you get pipeline-level cycle data
in seconds instead of hours.

## When to use

- User wants to profile pipeline behavior of an existing libnn kernel on
  Verilator and the full function is too slow to simulate.
- User wants a microbenchmark of a specific GEMM/conv/elementwise inner loop
  for cycle-accurate analysis or RTL bring-up.
- User asks "what's the inner kernel of `<fn>`" or "where does `<fn>` spend
  its cycles" -- the skill answers both, with a runnable artifact.

## When NOT to use

- For the FPGA cycle ground truth used by the `/optimize` loop -- that's
  whole-function measurement and is already automated by `/optimize`.
- For correctness debugging -- this skill produces a harness that exercises
  only the hot loop in isolation and does NOT verify outputs against golden.

## Quick start

```bash
# All-in-one: profile, find loop, emit harness + report
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/hot-inner-kernel-extractor/script"
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/extract_kernel.sh" <function_name>
```

Outputs land under `<skill>/work/<function_name>/`:
- `analysis.json` -- machine-readable: hot block PC, loop boundaries,
  live-ins, prologue, top hotblocks, etc.
- `analysis.md`   -- human-readable report with the loop body, live-in
  list, prologue, and a heuristic-init harness explainer
- `harness.S`     -- the standalone assembly harness, ready to assemble

## Pipeline (FSM-style, but executed straight through)

```
resolve_fn -> ensure_libnn_built -> profile_qemu_hotblocks
    -> analyze_objdump_loop -> emit_harness -> write_report
```

The skill is one-shot: each invocation produces a fresh artifact set. No
state persists across calls (unlike the `/optimize` skill, which has an FSM
across rounds).

### Step 1: resolve function

Given `<function_name>`, resolve to:
- The unit test name (strip `riscv_nn_` prefix if present)
- The unit test source under `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`

Implementation: `extract_kernel.sh` does the lookup. If the test doesn't
exist, the skill exits early with a clear error.

### Step 2: ensure libnn build artifacts

The skill needs:
- `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.a`
- `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<name>_perf.objdump` (from optimize/run_fpga_test.sh)

If either is missing, the skill calls into `optimize/script/build_libnn.sh`
and `optimize/script/run_fpga_test.sh` to produce them. This means the
skill REUSES the `/optimize` build infrastructure -- there's no parallel
build path to maintain.

### Step 3: profile via QEMU hotblocks

`run_hotblocks.sh <test>` compiles the unit test (or reuses a cached .adx),
runs it under QEMU with `-plugin libhotblocks.so,inline=true`, and dumps
`(pc, tcount, icount, ecount)` per translation block to a plugin log.

The hotblocks plugin counts every basic block QEMU sees, ranked by exec
count. The hottest block is the body of the innermost loop, regardless of
whether that loop is in the user's function or in a callee (e.g. a GEMM
sub-kernel). This matters for libnn because most conv functions dispatch
to `nn_mat_mult_kernel_*` -- the inner kernel is in the callee, not the
top-level conv.

### Step 4: analyze (find_hot_loop.py)

Inputs: hotblocks log + objdump + user's function name.

Algorithm:
1. Parse objdump into (functions, instructions). Each function has a PC
   range; each instruction is keyed by PC.
2. Compute the call graph reachable from the user's function (BFS over
   `jal`/`c.jal` targets that resolve to known symbols). This filters out
   the test harness's I/O loops, which would otherwise dominate ecount.
3. Pick the highest-ecount block whose containing function is in the
   reachable set.
4. From the hot PC, walk forward to find the innermost backward branch
   that closes a loop containing it. The branch's target is the loop
   header; the branch itself is the loop tail.
5. Live-in analysis: for each instruction in the loop body, classify its
   read/write registers. A register is a live-in if it is read before
   being written within the loop. RMW vector instructions (vd4dots,
   vmacc, vredsum, etc.) count their first operand as both read and
   written -- so accumulators show up as live-ins (they expect an initial
   value).
6. Walk the prologue (function entry .. loop header) backwards, keeping
   only instructions that produce live-in values (or values transitively
   consumed by such producers). This is the "producer subset" -- a
   pruned prologue.

Andes vendor extensions (`nds.vd4dots.vv`, `nds.lea.h`, etc.) are handled
by stripping the `nds.` prefix before classification.

### Step 5: emit harness (emit_harness.py)

Produces a `.S` file with:
- `.data` scratch buffers: `scratch_in`, `scratch_wt`, `scratch_out`
- `_start:`
  - small stack init
  - **live-in initialization** (heuristic): scalar pointer-like names
    point at `scratch_in`, scalar count-like names get `0x100`, vector
    accumulators (v0..v15) zeroed, vector activations (v16..v31) loaded
    from `scratch_in`
  - **warmup loop** (default 5 iters, unmeasured)
  - **measurement loop** (default 100 iters) wrapped in
    `csrr mcycle ... csrr mcycle`
  - cycle delta stored to a known symbol `cycle_total` for testbench
    scraping
  - `_exit:` spin label

Each measured iteration RESETS scalar live-ins (so pointers don't walk
off the buffer). Vector live-ins are NOT reset -- this is deliberate:
software-pipelined kernels that consume v_lo/produce v_hi (or vice
versa) need the body's writes to restore the inputs the next iter
expects.

The captured loop body is emitted **verbatim** from objdump, except the
backward branch at `loop_end_pc` is stripped (replaced by our outer
counter's `bnez`).

### Step 6: write report (write_report.py)

Renders `analysis.md` -- the user-facing artifact. Explains what was
found, why each piece was chosen, and how to assemble + run the harness.
Includes the loop body, live-in list, top hotblocks table, and prologue
fold-outs.

## Heuristic init -- read this before trusting the harness

The harness's live-in initialization is a heuristic. It works for the
common GEMM/conv inner loop pattern but can be wrong if:

- A scalar reg points at the *output* buffer (heuristic assumes input)
- A scalar reg is a *count* whose value drives a forward early-exit
  branch in the loop body (e.g. `blt rd, rs1, exit` -- if rd starts low
  the branch fires and the loop exits before we measure)
- A vector reg holds a *broadcast constant* (e.g. NN_ROUND, in_offset)
  rather than tile data

Always inspect the comment-flagged `# heuristic` lines in the generated
harness. The `analysis.md` report includes the verbatim prologue from
the function entry, which you can splice into the harness if heuristic
init produces wrong cycle counts.

## What the skill does NOT do

- Cycle-accurate simulation: that's Verilator's job. The skill produces
  the workload; you run it.
- Memory model fidelity: scratch buffers are flat arrays in `.data`. If
  your TB models DRAM/L2 latency, the cycle count includes those. If
  your TB uses TCM/single-cycle BRAM, you're measuring pipeline only --
  which is the intended use case for pipeline tuning.
- Correctness verification: the harness does not check outputs. Use
  `/optimize`'s verify step or a manual QEMU run for that.

## Files

```
hot-inner-kernel-extractor/
├── SKILL.md             -- this file
├── config.toml          -- symlink to ../optimize/config.toml
├── script/
│   ├── parse_config.sh  -- thin wrapper around optimize/parse_config.sh
│   ├── run_hotblocks.sh -- QEMU + hotblocks plugin runner
│   ├── find_hot_loop.py -- core analysis: hot block, loop, live-ins
│   ├── emit_harness.py  -- .S file emitter
│   ├── write_report.py  -- analysis.md renderer
│   └── extract_kernel.sh -- orchestrator
└── work/                -- per-function output dirs (gitignored)
```

## Rules

1. **Reuse `/optimize`'s build pipeline.** Never duplicate `build_libnn.sh`
   or `run_fpga_test.sh` -- delegate to them.
2. **Filter hotblocks by call graph.** Without filtering, the test harness's
   fread/memcmp loops dominate ecount and the wrong block is picked.
3. **Strip ONLY the backward branch from the loop body.** Keep mid-loop
   forward branches; they're guarded by live-in conditions and won't fire
   in steady state if init is correct.
4. **Reset scalar live-ins each measured iter, not vector live-ins.** The
   reason: SW-pipeline kernels need vector regs carried iter-to-iter.
5. **Heuristic init is a starting point.** Always remind the user (in the
   report) which lines need spot-checking.

---

# hot-inner-kernel-extractor

Produces a self-contained, cycle-measurable assembly harness from a libnn
function's hot inner loop. Designed for cases where running the full function
on a cycle-accurate simulator (Verilator at ~9 kHz, RTL emulators, etc.) is
too slow to reach steady state -- by extracting just the inner kernel and
wrapping it in a counted measurement loop, you get pipeline-level cycle data
in seconds instead of hours.

## When to use

- User wants to profile pipeline behavior of an existing libnn kernel on
  Verilator and the full function is too slow to simulate.
- User wants a microbenchmark of a specific GEMM/conv/elementwise inner loop
  for cycle-accurate analysis or RTL bring-up.
- User asks "what's the inner kernel of `<fn>`" or "where does `<fn>` spend
  its cycles" -- the skill answers both, with a runnable artifact.

## When NOT to use

- For the FPGA cycle ground truth used by the `/optimize` loop -- that's
  whole-function measurement and is already automated by `/optimize`.
- For correctness debugging -- this skill produces a harness that exercises
  only the hot loop in isolation and does NOT verify outputs against golden.

## Quick start

```bash
# All-in-one: profile, find loop, emit harness + report
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/hot-inner-kernel-extractor/script"
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/extract_kernel.sh" <function_name>
```

Outputs land under `<skill>/work/<function_name>/`:
- `analysis.json` -- machine-readable: hot block PC, loop boundaries,
  live-ins, prologue, top hotblocks, etc.
- `analysis.md`   -- human-readable report with the loop body, live-in
  list, prologue, and a heuristic-init harness explainer
- `harness.S`     -- the standalone assembly harness, ready to assemble

## Pipeline (FSM-style, but executed straight through)

```
resolve_fn -> ensure_libnn_built -> profile_qemu_hotblocks
    -> analyze_objdump_loop -> emit_harness -> write_report
```

The skill is one-shot: each invocation produces a fresh artifact set. No
state persists across calls (unlike the `/optimize` skill, which has an FSM
across rounds).

### Step 1: resolve function

Given `<function_name>`, resolve to:
- The unit test name (strip `riscv_nn_` prefix if present)
- The unit test source under `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`

Implementation: `extract_kernel.sh` does the lookup. If the test doesn't
exist, the skill exits early with a clear error.

### Step 2: ensure libnn build artifacts

The skill needs:
- `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.a`
- `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<name>_perf.objdump` (from optimize/run_fpga_test.sh)

If either is missing, the skill calls into `optimize/script/build_libnn.sh`
and `optimize/script/run_fpga_test.sh` to produce them. This means the
skill REUSES the `/optimize` build infrastructure -- there's no parallel
build path to maintain.

### Step 3: profile via QEMU hotblocks

`run_hotblocks.sh <test>` compiles the unit test (or reuses a cached .adx),
runs it under QEMU with `-plugin libhotblocks.so,inline=true`, and dumps
`(pc, tcount, icount, ecount)` per translation block to a plugin log.

The hotblocks plugin counts every basic block QEMU sees, ranked by exec
count. The hottest block is the body of the innermost loop, regardless of
whether that loop is in the user's function or in a callee (e.g. a GEMM
sub-kernel). This matters for libnn because most conv functions dispatch
to `nn_mat_mult_kernel_*` -- the inner kernel is in the callee, not the
top-level conv.

### Step 4: analyze (find_hot_loop.py)

Inputs: hotblocks log + objdump + user's function name.

Algorithm:
1. Parse objdump into (functions, instructions). Each function has a PC
   range; each instruction is keyed by PC.
2. Compute the call graph reachable from the user's function (BFS over
   `jal`/`c.jal` targets that resolve to known symbols). This filters out
   the test harness's I/O loops, which would otherwise dominate ecount.
3. Pick the highest-ecount block whose containing function is in the
   reachable set.
4. From the hot PC, walk forward to find the innermost backward branch
   that closes a loop containing it. The branch's target is the loop
   header; the branch itself is the loop tail.
5. Live-in analysis: for each instruction in the loop body, classify its
   read/write registers. A register is a live-in if it is read before
   being written within the loop. RMW vector instructions (vd4dots,
   vmacc, vredsum, etc.) count their first operand as both read and
   written -- so accumulators show up as live-ins (they expect an initial
   value).
6. Walk the prologue (function entry .. loop header) backwards, keeping
   only instructions that produce live-in values (or values transitively
   consumed by such producers). This is the "producer subset" -- a
   pruned prologue.

Andes vendor extensions (`nds.vd4dots.vv`, `nds.lea.h`, etc.) are handled
by stripping the `nds.` prefix before classification.

### Step 5: emit harness (emit_harness.py)

Produces a `.S` file with:
- `.data` scratch buffers: `scratch_in`, `scratch_wt`, `scratch_out`
- `_start:`
  - small stack init
  - **live-in initialization** (heuristic): scalar pointer-like names
    point at `scratch_in`, scalar count-like names get `0x100`, vector
    accumulators (v0..v15) zeroed, vector activations (v16..v31) loaded
    from `scratch_in`
  - **warmup loop** (default 5 iters, unmeasured)
  - **measurement loop** (default 100 iters) wrapped in
    `csrr mcycle ... csrr mcycle`
  - cycle delta stored to a known symbol `cycle_total` for testbench
    scraping
  - `_exit:` spin label

Each measured iteration RESETS scalar live-ins (so pointers don't walk
off the buffer). Vector live-ins are NOT reset -- this is deliberate:
software-pipelined kernels that consume v_lo/produce v_hi (or vice
versa) need the body's writes to restore the inputs the next iter
expects.

The captured loop body is emitted **verbatim** from objdump, except the
backward branch at `loop_end_pc` is stripped (replaced by our outer
counter's `bnez`).

### Step 6: write report (write_report.py)

Renders `analysis.md` -- the user-facing artifact. Explains what was
found, why each piece was chosen, and how to assemble + run the harness.
Includes the loop body, live-in list, top hotblocks table, and prologue
fold-outs.

## Heuristic init -- read this before trusting the harness

The harness's live-in initialization is a heuristic. It works for the
common GEMM/conv inner loop pattern but can be wrong if:

- A scalar reg points at the *output* buffer (heuristic assumes input)
- A scalar reg is a *count* whose value drives a forward early-exit
  branch in the loop body (e.g. `blt rd, rs1, exit` -- if rd starts low
  the branch fires and the loop exits before we measure)
- A vector reg holds a *broadcast constant* (e.g. NN_ROUND, in_offset)
  rather than tile data

Always inspect the comment-flagged `# heuristic` lines in the generated
harness. The `analysis.md` report includes the verbatim prologue from
the function entry, which you can splice into the harness if heuristic
init produces wrong cycle counts.

## What the skill does NOT do

- Cycle-accurate simulation: that's Verilator's job. The skill produces
  the workload; you run it.
- Memory model fidelity: scratch buffers are flat arrays in `.data`. If
  your TB models DRAM/L2 latency, the cycle count includes those. If
  your TB uses TCM/single-cycle BRAM, you're measuring pipeline only --
  which is the intended use case for pipeline tuning.
- Correctness verification: the harness does not check outputs. Use
  `/optimize`'s verify step or a manual QEMU run for that.

## Files

```
hot-inner-kernel-extractor/
├── SKILL.md             -- this file
├── config.toml          -- symlink to ../optimize/config.toml
├── script/
│   ├── parse_config.sh  -- thin wrapper around optimize/parse_config.sh
│   ├── run_hotblocks.sh -- QEMU + hotblocks plugin runner
│   ├── find_hot_loop.py -- core analysis: hot block, loop, live-ins
│   ├── emit_harness.py  -- .S file emitter
│   ├── write_report.py  -- analysis.md renderer
│   └── extract_kernel.sh -- orchestrator
└── work/                -- per-function output dirs (gitignored)
```

## Rules

1. **Reuse `/optimize`'s build pipeline.** Never duplicate `build_libnn.sh`
   or `run_fpga_test.sh` -- delegate to them.
2. **Filter hotblocks by call graph.** Without filtering, the test harness's
   fread/memcmp loops dominate ecount and the wrong block is picked.
3. **Strip ONLY the backward branch from the loop body.** Keep mid-loop
   forward branches; they're guarded by live-in conditions and won't fire
   in steady state if init is correct.
4. **Reset scalar live-ins each measured iter, not vector live-ins.** The
   reason: SW-pipeline kernels need vector regs carried iter-to-iter.
5. **Heuristic init is a starting point.** Always remind the user (in the
   report) which lines need spot-checking.

- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. - iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions

libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the

- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. #!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Thin wrapper that delegates to the optimize skill's parse_config.sh.
#
# Why delegate: this skill shares toolchain/QEMU/CFLAGS/work-dir with the
# /optimize FSM. Maintaining one config source eliminates drift -- if the user
# updates a flag in optimize/config.toml, this skill picks it up automatically.
#
# The wrapper also exports a few CFG_HIK_* variables specific to this skill:
#   CFG_HIK_SKILL_DIR     - this skill's root directory
#   CFG_HIK_WORK_DIR      - per-skill work dir (kernel artifacts, harness, etc.)
#   CFG_HIK_QEMU_PLUGIN   - absolute path to libhotblocks.so
################################################################################
set -uo pipefail

_hik_self_dir="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
_hik_skill_dir="$(cd "${_hik_self_dir}/.." && pwd)"

# config.toml lives at <skill>/config.toml (symlinked to optimize/config.toml).
# The optimize parser resolves [target.uarch_ref] etc. relative to the SKILL
# directory of whatever owns the config -- since we symlink, we want those
# paths to resolve against the optimize skill (where fpga_config/ etc. live),
# not this skill. Easiest fix: point CONFIG_TOML at the *real* file.
_hik_config_toml="$(readlink -f "${_hik_skill_dir}/config.toml")"

# Source the optimize parser explicitly so its skill_root computation lands in
# the optimize directory (sibling resolution for fpga_config/, references/).
_hik_optimize_parse="$(cd "${_hik_skill_dir}/../optimize/script" && pwd)/parse_config.sh"
if [ ! -f "${_hik_optimize_parse}" ]; then
    echo "parse_config.sh: cannot find optimize parser at ${_hik_optimize_parse}" >&2
    return 1 2>/dev/null || exit 1
fi

# shellcheck disable=SC1090
source "${_hik_optimize_parse}" "${_hik_config_toml}"

# Skill-specific exports
export CFG_HIK_SKILL_DIR="${_hik_skill_dir}"
export CFG_HIK_WORK_DIR="${_hik_skill_dir}/work"

# QEMU plugin path: derive from QEMU binary location (bin/../contrib/plugins).
# This works for upstream and Andes QEMU builds; users override by setting
# CFG_HIK_QEMU_PLUGIN before sourcing.
if [ -z "${CFG_HIK_QEMU_PLUGIN:-}" ]; then
    _hik_qemu_root="$(cd "$(dirname "${CFG_QEMU_BIN}")/.." && pwd)"
    CFG_HIK_QEMU_PLUGIN="${_hik_qemu_root}/contrib/plugins/libhotblocks.so"
fi
export CFG_HIK_QEMU_PLUGIN

mkdir -p "${CFG_HIK_WORK_DIR}"

unset _hik_self_dir _hik_skill_dir _hik_config_toml _hik_optimize_parse _hik_qemu_root

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- hot-inner-kernel-extractor: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch
